body:
The "Get Started" button in the navbar was still visible and clickable when the user was already on the /upload page. Clicking it would silently reload the same page, making it appear broken to users.
Fix: Used useLocation() hook to detect the current route and conditionally hide the "Get Started" button when the user is already on /upload.

title: ## [Feature / Roadmap] End-to-End Automation Pipeline: React + Express + Python AI Workflow
body:
### 📸 About snappass-ai
**snappass-ai** automates the tedious, manual workflow traditionally faced by photo studios, cyber cafes, and print shops. Currently, preparing a passport or ID photo—cropping, centering faces, removing backgrounds, adjusting exact dimensions, and manually tiling copies onto an A4 sheet—takes 15–20 minutes per customer. 

This platform automates the entire process in seconds, drastically reducing human error, accelerating business turnaround times, and ensuring absolute printing consistency.

---

### 🛠️ Core Contribution Tracks

We are establishing the core architecture and looking for contributors to claim and implement the following modules. Please comment below to be assigned to a specific track:

#### 1. 🐍 Python AI Engine (Core Image Processing)
* **Face Centering & Cropping:** Implement precise facial landmark detection (e.g., using MediaPipe or Dlib) to automatically isolate, center, and crop faces according to standard ID specifications.
* **Background Removal:** Integrate a lightweight, efficient background matting or segmentation model (e.g., Rembg, BiRefNet) to ensure clean cutouts.
* **A4 Sheet Grid Tiling:** Write the automation script (using Pillow/OpenCV) that perfectly arranges multiple copies of the processed ID photo onto a standard A4 print layout with optimal margin spacing.

#### 2. ⚡ Express Backend (API & Orchestration Layer)
* **File Upload & Queueing:** Set up secure endpoints to receive raw images and handle temporary storage or stream processing cleanly.
* **Python Bridge:** Implement a robust mechanism (e.g., `child_process` or a microservice API layer) to hand off images to the Python AI engine and handle the returned asset URLs/buffers.
* **Dimension Templates:** Create a configuration structure supporting various global standard dimensions (e.g., US 2x2 inches, Schengen visa, Indian passport size).

#### 3. 🎨 React Frontend (UI/UX Layer)
* **Intuitive Upload & Preview:** Build a responsive interface where shop owners can upload images and select the target passport/visa region template.
* **Interactive Grid Layout Preview:** Show a real-time layout preview of how the photos will fit onto the final A4 sheet before clicking print.
* **Print-Ready Export:** Implement a clean "One-Click Print" functionality or high-resolution PDF download wrapper.

---

### 🤝 How to Get Started

1. **Comment below** indicating which track you want to work on (e.g., *Track 1: Python AI Engine - Face Centering* or *Track 3: React Frontend Layout Preview*).
2. If you have a specific technical package or library in mind for your implementation, please mention it in your comment.
3. A maintainer will assign you to the issue or break out a dedicated sub-issue for your specific task.

Let's save thousands of hours for local print shops and photo studios world-wide! 🚀🖼️

title: Feat: Add multi-recipient sharing with individual revocation control
body:
## Summary

Implements a comprehensive multi-recipient password sharing system with individual access control and revocation capabilities. Users can now share passwords with multiple recipients simultaneously while maintaining independent control over each recipient's access, including the ability to revoke access for specific individuals without affecting others.

## Changes

### Database Models

**PasswordShare Model** (`src/models/PasswordShare.js`)
- Represents a password share for multiple recipients
- Tracks share metadata (owner, expiration, access limits)
- Supports per-share access count limits
- Maintains IP address log and total access tracking

**ShareRecipient Model** (`src/models/ShareRecipient.js`)
- Tracks individual recipient access and control
- Per-recipient unique access tokens for security
- Independent status tracking (pending, accepted, accessed, revoked)
- Detailed access history with IP and user agent logging
- Individual revocation tracking with reason and timestamp

### Service Layer

**MultiRecipientService** (`src/services/multiRecipientService.js`) provides:
- `createMultiRecipientShare()` - Create share for up to 100 recipients
- `addRecipients()` - Add recipients to existing active share
- `revokeRecipient()` - Revoke individual recipient access
- `revokeAllRecipients()` - Revoke all recipients at once
- `recordAccess()` - Log access with IP/user agent tracking
- `checkAccessStatus()` - Verify access validity
- `getShareDetails()` - Retrieve complete share information with stats
- `getAccessAnalytics()` - Generate analytics and access timeline
- `cleanupExpiredShares()` - Automated cleanup of expired shares

### Key Features

**Individual Revocation**
- Revoke specific recipient without affecting others
- Revocation reason and timestamp tracked
- Independent status per recipient
- Audit trail for compliance

**Access Control**
- Unique access token per recipient
- Per-recipient access count limits
- Per-share global access limits

- Access status validation

**Access Tracking**

**Recipient Management**
- Add recipients to existing active shares

- Bulk revocation capability
- Email uniqueness per share

**Scalability**
- MongoDB indexing for performance
- Efficient query patterns

- Batch operations
- Automatic cleanup of expired data

### Test Coverage

Comprehensive test suite with 20+ test cases covering:
- Multi-recipient share creation with validation
- Recipient addition with limits
- Individual recipient revocation
- Bulk revocation operations
- Access tracking and recording
- Access count limit enforcement

- Status transitions

- Database schema specifications
- RESTful endpoint documentation
- Request/response examples
- Error handling guidelines
- Integration examples
- Security best practices
- Feature overview
- Scalability considerations

## Security Considerations

- Each recipient receives a unique cryptographically random access token (32 bytes)
- Passwords encrypted in database
- No plaintext password storage
- IP address and user agent tracking for anomaly detection
- Automatic expiration enforced
- Revocation is immediate and irreversible
- Per-recipient access limits prevent abuse
- Integrates with password content validation from issue #697

## Integration Ready

The service layer is designed for easy integration with Express.js controllers:

```javascript
const service = require('./services/multiRecipientService');

const share = await service.createMultiRecipientShare({
  encryptedPassword: encrypt(password),
  expirationMinutes: 120,
  recipients: ['user1@example.com', 'user2@example.com'],
  maxAccessCount: 10,
  description: 'Database credentials'
});

await service.revokeRecipient(share.share._id, 'user1@example.com', userId, 'Left team');
```

title: Fix: Add comprehensive password content validation to prevent malware distribution
body:
## Summary

Implements multi-layer password content validation to detect and prevent malware distribution through password sharing. This addresses the security gap where users could inadvertently share malicious content (executables, scripts, URLs) disguised as passwords.

## Changes

### New Validators & Middleware
- **PasswordContentValidator** (`src/validation/passwordContent.validation.js`): Core validation engine with 20+ security rules
  - Detects executable files (.exe, .bat, .sh, .jar, .zip, etc.)
  - Identifies URLs masquerading as passwords
  - Blocks script protocols (javascript:, vbscript:, data:)
  - Rejects file system paths
  - Prevents blocked dangerous commands (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, rm -rf, etc.)
  - Calculates security levels (high/medium/low)
  - Detects homograph attacks using similar-looking characters

- **validatePasswordContent middleware** (`src/middleware/validatePasswordContent.js`): Express middleware for easy integration into password sharing endpoints
  - Validates password content before sharing
  - Returns detailed error and warning messages
  - Attaches security metadata to response context

### Comprehensive Test Coverage
- **28 test cases** covering all validation scenarios
  - Valid password acceptance
  - Executable file rejection
  - URL detection and blocking
  - Script/protocol blocking
  - File path rejection
  - Blocked content patterns
  - Size limit enforcement (10KB)
  - Warning patterns (URL-like, IP addresses)
  - Security level calculation
  - Edge cases (empty, null, unicode)

## How to Integrate

Add middleware to password sharing routes:

```javascript
const validatePasswordContent = require('./middleware/validatePasswordContent');

app.post('/api/share-password', 
  validatePasswordContent,
  sharePasswordController
);
```

## Validation Features

- **Malware Prevention**: Blocks attempts to share executables and scripts
- **User Warnings**: Alerts for suspicious content (IP addresses, URL-like patterns)
- **Security Scoring**: Evaluates password strength
- **Homograph Detection**: Warns about lookalike character combinations (0/O, 1/l/I, 8/B)
- **Size Validation**: Enforces 10KB maximum password length

## Testing

All tests pass with comprehensive coverage:
- Valid credentials accepted
- Malicious patterns blocked
- Warnings generated for suspicious content
- Edge cases handled properly

Resolves #697

title: validateImageBuffer middleware is defined but never registered on file upload route
body:
### Problem
`validateImageBuffer` in `upload.middleware.js` is designed to verify file magic bytes, but it is never registered in `upload.routes.js`. Attackers can upload non-image malicious payloads simply by renaming the extension to `.png`.

### Expected
Add the `validateImageBuffer` middleware to the upload route.

title: Anonymous uploads leak storage space due to missing user association check in Upload model
body:
### Problem
In `upload.model.js`, the `user` field is marked as `required: true`. Because anonymous uploads do not have a user id, `Upload.create` is skipped. This means anonymous uploads are never registered in the database, making it impossible to track, manage, or clean them up.

### Expected
Make the `user` field in `Upload` schema optional, allowing all uploads (including anonymous ones) to be recorded in the database.

title: Stored XSS vulnerability in input sanitization middleware via nested tags
body:
### Problem
In `backend/src/middleware/sanitize.middleware.js`, the XSS sanitization uses the regex `/<[^>]*>/g` to strip HTML tags. This can be easily bypassed using nested HTML tags like `<<script>script>alert(1)</script>`, leading to Stored XSS.

### Expected
HTML encode dangerous characters (like `<` and `>` to `<` and `>`) instead of naively removing them with a regex.

title: Clean up unused validate_file_magic and dead face_validator module
body:
### Problem
In `python-ai-service`, `main.py` contains `validate_file_magic` which is defined but never called, and `face_validator.py` contains `validate_image_bytes` which is also never called. This contributes to codebase bloat.

### Expected
Remove the dead code and clean up the unused files.

title: Missing image resolution boundary check in face quality check
body:
### Problem
The `assess_face_quality` endpoint loads user-provided images directly into OpenCV via `cv2.imread` without validating their resolution. If a user uploads an extremely large image (e.g. a pixel-bomb), the process could run out of memory and crash.

### Expected
Use lightweight PIL metadata header extraction (`Image.open(path).size`) to check image dimensions first. Reject images with resolution exceeding 8000x8000 pixels.

title: Caching middleware incorrectly caches error response JSONs
body:
### Problem
`cacheMiddleware` in `cache.middleware.js` intercepts `res.json` and caches responses unconditionally. If a database timeout or a validation failure occurs, the error response is cached and served to subsequent valid requests.

### Expected
Only cache responses when `res.statusCode` is in the success range (200-299).

title: Insecure WebP magic bytes matching in face_validator
body:
### Problem
In `python-ai-service/app/services/face_validator.py`, WebP magic bytes are validated as `b'RIFF': 'webp'`. However, any file starting with `RIFF` (e.g. WAV audio or AVI video) is misidentified as a WebP image, which is insecure.

### Expected
Verify both the `RIFF` signature in the first 4 bytes and the `WEBP` header at bytes 8-12.

title: Testimonials API lacks limit and pagination constraints
body:
### Problem
`GET /api/testimonials` loads all testimonials from the database via `findApprovedTestimonials()`. If the number of reviews increases significantly, this will cause memory exhaustion and slow response times.

### Expected
The testimonials API should support pagination (`page` and `limit` query parameters), defaulting to limit 20 and maximum 100, sorted newest first.

title: Incorrect Content-Type and extension for A4 sheet downloads
body:
### Problem
In `print.controller.js`, `generateSheet` sets the response header to `Content-Type: image/png` and returns a file with a `.png` extension.
However, the Python service generates a JPEG file (`canvas.save(output_path, "JPEG")`). Serving JPEG bytes with a PNG content type is incorrect.

### Expected
The controller should set `Content-Type: image/jpeg` and return the file with a `.jpg` extension.

title: Cloudinary cleanup deletes local file immediately, breaking subsequent image processing
body:
### Problem
In `backend/src/controllers/upload.controller.js`, if Cloudinary is configured, it uploads the file and immediately calls `fs.unlinkSync(localPath)`.
However, `image.controller.js` and `print.controller.js` expect the file to exist locally at `filePath`. Consequently, any call to `/api/process` or `/api/print/generate-sheet` fails with `404 File not found on server`.

### Expected
The backend should check if the file exists locally, and if not (and Cloudinary is active), download the file from the Cloudinary URL to the local `uploads` directory before sending it to the Python AI service, deleting it afterward.

title: [FEATURE] Improve Footer Navigation with Quick Actions
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

The current footer navigation relies primarily on plain text links, making it difficult for users to quickly identify important actions and sections. The lack of visual hierarchy, grouping, and clear distinction between navigation categories can reduce usability, especially on mobile devices.

---

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Redesign the footer navigation by introducing modern quick-action sections and improved visual organization.

Key improvements:
- Add relevant icons alongside important footer links.
- Group related links into visually distinct cards or sections.
- Improve spacing, alignment, and typography for better readability.
- Enhance responsiveness across desktop, tablet, and mobile devices.
- Introduce clearer visual hierarchy to help users quickly find important resources.

---

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

- Keeping the existing text-only footer and making minor spacing adjustments.
- Adding separators between links without changing the overall layout.
- Using dropdown sections for footer navigation.

However, a card-based footer with icons and better grouping provides a cleaner, more intuitive, and modern user experience.

---

**Additional context**

The redesign should maintain the existing functionality and links while improving presentation. Inspiration can be taken from modern SaaS and product landing pages where footer content is organized into clearly defined sections with icons, better spacing, and stronger visual hierarchy.

title: [FEATURE] Add "Back to Top" Button
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Many pages within SnapPass AI, such as the Terms & Conditions page, contain lengthy content that requires significant scrolling. Once users reach the bottom of the page, they must manually scroll all the way back to the top, which can be inconvenient and negatively impact the user experience, especially on mobile devices.

---

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Add a floating **"Back to Top"** button that appears after the user scrolls beyond a certain threshold. When clicked, the button should smoothly scroll the page back to the top.

Key requirements:
- Show the button only after scrolling down a predefined distance.
- Smooth scrolling animation back to the top.
- Responsive design for desktop, tablet, and mobile devices.
- Accessible implementation with proper ARIA labels and keyboard support.
- Automatically hide when the user is already at the top of the page.

---

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

- Relying on users to manually scroll back to the top.
- Using browser-specific shortcuts such as the Home key.
- Adding duplicate navigation links at the bottom of long pages.

However, a dedicated floating button provides a more intuitive and user-friendly solution across all devices.

---

**Additional context**

This feature would be particularly beneficial on content-heavy pages such as:
- Terms & Conditions
- Privacy Policy
- Help & Documentation pages

The button should follow the existing SnapPass AI design language and remain unobtrusive while improving navigation and accessibility.

title: [Enhancement][Good First Issue]: Add Password Copy-to-Clipboard Feature with Visual Feedback State
body:
##Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.
I am frustrated when generating a secure, self-destructing password link or retrieving a shared secret because I have to manually highlight, select, and copy the text string. This manual process is prone to accidental mis-selections (missing a character) and slows down the user flow on both desktop and mobile devices.

##Describe the solution you'd like
I would like to see a dedicated "Copy to Clipboard" button (using a modern clipboard icon) positioned directly next to the generated URL or secret text display fields.

Clicking this button should use the native browser navigator.clipboard.writeText() API to instantly copy the text.

To ensure a good user experience, the button should provide immediate visual feedback (e.g., changing the icon to a checkmark ✔️ or displaying a brief "Copied!" tooltip/toast notification for 1.5 to 2 seconds) before reverting to its original state.

##Describe alternatives you've considered
An alternative would be leaving the UI as-is, requiring users to manually select and copy text, or using an automatic text-selection on-click action. However, a dedicated copy button with active visual feedback is the standard industry practice for modern credential managers and security tools, offering the most seamless experience.

##Additional context

This enhancement will be implemented using the repository's existing UI styling framework (e.g., Tailwind CSS) to keep the design highly consistent.

This is a non-duplicate feature enhancement that helps improve the frontend accessibility and accessibility standards.

##Note for Maintainers: I am participating in GSSoC and would love to work on implementing this feature. Please assign this issue to me!

Users lacked fine-grained control over color temperature, edge detail, and tonal range, which are essential for any photo editing workflow. ## Screenshots --- ## Solution Introduce 4 new canvas-based adjustment sliders: | Tool | What it does | |---|---| | Warmth | Adjusts color temperature (cool → warm) | | Sharpness | Edge enhancement via canvas convolution | | Shadows | Lifts or deepens shadow regions | | Highlights | Recovers or boosts highlight areas | --- ## Scope **New tools (this issue):** Warmth · Sharpness · Shadows · Highlights **Existing tools (unchanged):** Crop · Brightness · Contrast · Saturation --- ## Technical approach - New file: `frontend/src/utils/imageAdjustments.js` — shared canvas render pipeline - Preview and download use the same render function (WYSIWYG) - Slider changes debounced at ~120ms for large image performance - Toolbar scrolls horizontally on smaller screens --- ## Acceptance criteria - Warmth slider adjusts color temperature visibly - Sharpness slider sharpens edges on the canvas - Shadows slider lifts/deepens dark regions - Highlights slider boosts/recovers bright regions - Downloaded image matches the canvas preview exactly - Works on mobile (horizontal scroll toolbar)

title: Add Character Counter and Input Limit Feedback for Prompt Field
body:
Description

Currently, the prompt input field does not provide any indication of how many characters have been entered or whether a maximum limit exists. Users may unintentionally exceed supported input lengths or be unsure about prompt size constraints.

Expected Behavior

Display a live character counter below or beside the prompt input field.
Show the current count and maximum allowed characters (e.g., 120/200).
Provide a visual warning when the user approaches the limit (for example, at 80–90% of the maximum length).
Prevent users from entering text beyond the defined limit.
Display a clear validation message if the limit is reached.

Benefits

Improves user experience by providing real-time feedback.
Prevents submission errors caused by overly long prompts.
Makes prompt length constraints transparent.
Enhances form validation and accessibility.

Possible Implementation

Track input length using component state.
Use the HTML maxLength attribute or equivalent validation.
Change counter color when nearing the limit.
Add an accessible validation message for screen readers.

title: fix: prevent unlimited anonymous reviews from manipulating average rating
body:
> 🌟 This PR is part of **GirlScript Summer of Code 2026 (GSSoC '26)**

## Summary
Fixes #700

Fixes the issue where unlimited anonymous review submissions could inflate 
or deflate the displayed average rating. Previously, testimonials were stored 
only in `localStorage` with no server-side checks, so the same user could 
submit multiple reviews under different names and each one affected the average.

This PR moves reviews to a shared backend API and adds spam-prevention controls 
so ratings reflect genuine feedback more reliably.

Also fixes backend startup crash by replacing the stub user.model.js with a proper Mongoose model, required for the server to run and serve the testimonials API.

## Changes

### Backend
- Added `Testimonial` MongoDB model with approval status and client fingerprint tracking
- New API routes:
  - `GET /api/testimonials` — fetch approved reviews + average rating stats
  - `POST /api/testimonials` — submit a new review (rate-limited)
  - `PUT /api/testimonials` — update an existing review for the same device
- Spam prevention:
  - **One review per device** via unique `clientFingerprint`
  - **IP rate limiting** (5 attempts/hour)
  - **Daily IP cap** (max 3 new reviews per IP per 24h)
  - **Duplicate comment detection** (same IP + same comment within 24h)
  - **Honeypot field** to block basic bots
  - Server-side validation for name, rating, and comment length
- Seeded default testimonials on first load when the collection is empty

### Frontend
- Replaced `localStorage`-only review storage with `testimonialService` API integration
- Persistent browser fingerprint (`reviewClient.js`) to enforce one review per device
- Users who already submitted see **Edit Your Review** instead of creating duplicates
- Added success/error messaging and bilingual form labels (EN/HI)
- Graceful fallback to sample testimonials if the API is unavailable (read-only)

## Why This Approach
| Approach | Benefit |
|----------|---------|
| Server-side storage | Shared, consistent ratings for all users |
| Fingerprint uniqueness | Stops casual repeat submissions from the same browser |
| Edit-in-place (PUT) | Users can update their review without adding a new one |
| IP limits + honeypot | Reduces automated spam and rating manipulation |
| Backend validation | Client-side checks alone can be bypassed |

## Test Plan
- [ ] Start backend with MongoDB running
- [ ] Set `VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:5005/api` in `frontend/.env`
- [ ] Open homepage testimonials section — seed reviews load with average rating
- [ ] Submit a review → success message, rating updates
- [ ] Try submitting again with a different name → blocked with message to edit existing review
- [ ] Click **Edit Your Review** → update rating/comment → changes save successfully
- [ ] Verify average rating only changes when a review is added/updated, not duplicated
- [ ] Confirm honeypot: filling hidden `website` field rejects submission (API test)
- [ ] Confirm UI still shows sample reviews when backend is offline (fallback mode)

## Notes
- Review submission requires the backend API; fallback mode is read-only
- Future improvement: optional admin moderation queue (`pending` → `approved`) 
  if moderation UI is added later

## Type of Change
- [x] Bug fix
- [x] New feature (spam prevention, fingerprinting)
- [ ] Breaking change

title: Bug: Unlimited Anonymous Reviews Can Manipulate Average Rating
body:
**Bug: Unlimited Anonymous Reviews Can Manipulate Average Rating**

**Description**

The current review system allows unlimited anonymous review submissions without any validation or restriction mechanism. As a result, a single individual can submit multiple reviews, causing the displayed average rating to be artificially increased or decreased.

Since each review contributes to the overall rating calculation, repeated submissions can significantly impact the reliability and credibility of the review system.

**Steps to Reproduce**

1. Navigate to the Reviews/Testimonial section.
2. Submit a review with any rating.
3. Submit another review using a different name.
4. Observe that the new review is accepted successfully.
5. Repeat the process multiple times.
6. Notice that each submission affects the displayed average rating.

 **Expected Behavior**

* The review system should prevent duplicate or excessive submissions from the same source.
* Reviews should accurately represent genuine user feedback.
* A mechanism should exist to reduce review spam and rating manipulation.

**Actual Behavior**

* Unlimited reviews can be submitted anonymously.
* Every submitted review is included in the average rating calculation.
* The displayed rating can be manipulated through repeated submissions.

 **Impact**

* Average ratings become inaccurate and misleading.
* Users may make decisions based on manipulated ratings.
* Trust in the review system decreases.
* The platform becomes vulnerable to review spam.

**Suggested Solution**

Possible approaches include:

* Restricting multiple submissions from the same source.
* Implementing email or account-based review verification.
* Allowing users to edit existing reviews instead of creating new ones.
* Adding server-side validation and spam-prevention measures.

**Additional Notes**

This issue directly affects the accuracy and trustworthiness of the platform's review and rating system.

title: Bug: Shared password content not validated, malware distribution vector
body:
## Problem Statement
Shared password content not validated, enabling malware distribution:
- User pastes malware URL as password
- Other users download executable thinking it's a credential
- Becomes attack vector for credential stealers

**Business Impact:** Platform liability, legal consequences, reputational damage, malware distribution platform
**Technical Impact:** No content validation, no sandboxing

## Root Cause Analysis
No validation of password field content. Accepts any string including executable paths, URLs, scripts. No malware scanning performed.

## Solution Overview
Implement content validation:
1. Size limit: max 10KB per password
2. Character validation: exclude suspicious patterns
3. URL detection: warn if password looks like URL
4. Executable detection: block .exe, .sh, .bat files
5. Malware scanning: integrate VirusTotal API for suspicious content
6. User warning: This looks like a URL, did you mean to share a password?
7. Logging: track flagged passwords for review

## Type of Change
- Security fix: Content validation
- Feature: Malware protection

## Testing Done
- Unit test: Character validation rules
- Malware test: Known malware signatures blocked
- Edge case: Unicode, homograph attacks
- Performance test: Validation adds < 50ms
- False positive test: Valid passwords not blocked

## Related Issue
Relates to OWASP A05: Security Misconfiguration

## Suggested Labels
security, validation, malware-protection, gssoc26

title: Enhancement: Add multi-recipient sharing with individual revocation control
body:
## Problem Statement
Current system supports only single-recipient sharing. Required use case:
- Share credential with team of 5 people
- Revoke access from one person without affecting others
- Track which team member accessed the credential

**Business Impact:** Limited to single-use scenarios, enterprise adoption blocked
**Technical Impact:** Current architecture doesn't support multiple share records per password

## Root Cause Analysis
Schema design uses one-to-one relationship between password and share token. No support for multiple recipients or access tracking per recipient.

## Solution Overview
Redesign for multi-recipient support:
1. New schema: Share (1) -> Recipients (N)
2. Individual revocation: revoke_recipient(share_id, recipient_id)
3. Per-recipient tracking: access_log(recipient_id, timestamp)
4. Group sharing: create_group_share(password_id, recipients=[])
5. Notification: email each recipient with unique link
6. Dashboard: see who has access, who accessed, when

## Type of Change
- Feature: Multi-recipient sharing
- Enhancement: Team collaboration

## Testing Done
- Unit test: Multiple recipients sharing
- Revocation test: Revoke one doesn't affect others
- Access tracking test: Individual access logs
- Email test: Each recipient gets unique link
- Performance test: Group sharing with 100+ recipients

## Related Issue
Relates to enterprise features and team collaboration

## Suggested Labels
feature, enhancement, team-collaboration, gssoc26

title: Bug: Access logs stored plaintext, exposing who viewed which password
body:
## Problem Statement
Access logs stored in plaintext expose viewing history:
- Attacker gains DB access: sees who viewed which passwords and when
- Compliance failure: GDPR requires log protection
- Information leak: Reveals sensitive metadata about password usage

**Business Impact:** Privacy violation, regulatory penalties (GDPR/CCPA), loss of compliance certifications
**Technical Impact:** No encryption at rest, no access control on logs

## Root Cause Analysis
Access logs stored as plaintext in database with no encryption. Log retention policy undefined. Insufficient access controls on log tables.

## Solution Overview
Implement log protection:
1. Encrypt logs at rest using AES-256-GCM
2. Add log table access controls: only support staff
3. Implement log retention policy: auto-delete after 90 days
4. Hash IP addresses in logs (use bcrypt)
5. Separate log tables: read-only replica for analytics
6. Audit trail: log who accessed the logs

## Type of Change
- Security fix: Data protection
- Compliance: GDPR/CCPA alignment

## Testing Done
- Encryption test: Verify plaintext never stored
- Access control test: Non-authorized users cannot query logs
- Retention test: Logs deleted after 90 days
- Performance test: Log encryption overhead < 5%
- Compliance test: Meet GDPR article 32 requirements

## Related Issue
Relates to OWASP A01: Broken Access Control, A04: Insecure Design

## Suggested Labels
security, privacy, encryption, compliance, gssoc26

title: Bug: Shared passwords never expire, indefinite access after sharing
body:
## Problem Statement
Shared passwords never expire, creating indefinite access windows:
- Recipient loses access to sharing context after days
- Forgotten share links remain active for months
- Compromised shares cannot be revoked without recipient knowledge

**Business Impact:** Extended exposure window for compromised credentials, inability to control access lifecycle
**Technical Impact:** No TTL mechanism, no revocation capability

## Root Cause Analysis
Share tokens lack expiration timestamp. Database schema has no expires_at field. No cleanup job removes expired shares.

## Solution Overview
Implement expiration and revocation:
1. Add expires_at timestamp (default 24h, configurable)
2. Add revoked_at field for explicit revocation
3. Check expiration on every password retrieval
4. Return 410 Gone for expired shares
5. Provide revoke endpoint: DELETE /api/share/{token}
6. Email notification: Your shared password expired
7. Cleanup job: Delete expired shares daily

## Type of Change
- Security fix: Access control lifecycle
- Feature: Expiration and revocation

## Testing Done
- TTL test: Verify expiration after configured duration
- Revocation test: Explicit revoke prevents access
- Edge case: Expired token returns proper HTTP status
- Cleanup test: Expired shares removed by job
- API test: Revoke endpoint accessible to share creator

## Related Issue
Relates to OWASP A01: Broken Access Control

## Suggested Labels
security, access-control, feature, gssoc26

title: Security: Share tokens are predictable, enabling password enumeration attacks
body:
## Problem Statement
Share tokens use weak random generation allowing attacker enumeration. Attacker can:
- Brute force valid tokens with 10,000 attempts
- Extract shared passwords before intended recipients
- Perform mass password harvesting

**Business Impact:** User privacy breach, legal liability, credential theft, loss of customer trust
**Technical Impact:** Cryptographic weakness, no rate limiting on token validation

## Root Cause Analysis
Tokens generated using random.randint() or predictable pseudo-random sources. Token space insufficient (< 2^64). No cryptographic randomness used.

## Solution Overview
Implement secure token generation:
1. Use secrets module for cryptographic randomness
2. Generate 256-bit tokens (32 bytes): secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
3. Apply rate limiting: max 5 failed attempts per minute per IP
4. Log all token validation attempts
5. Add CAPTCHA after 3 failed attempts
6. Token entropy verification test: min 128 bits

## Type of Change
- Security fix: Cryptographic vulnerability
- Enhancement: Rate limiting

## Testing Done
- Entropy test: Generate 10,000 tokens, verify uniqueness
- Brute force test: Simulate 10,000 guesses, verify all blocked
- Rate limit test: Exceed limits, verify enforcement
- Backward compatibility: Existing tokens still work
- Performance test: Token generation < 1ms

## Related Issue
Relates to OWASP A02: Cryptographic Failures

## Suggested Labels
security, critical, cryptography, gssoc26

title: [FEATURE] Add processing queue and job history for image generation tasks
body:
## Feature Description
Currently, when a user uploads and processes an image using AI, there is no system to track ongoing or completed processing tasks. If the user navigates away or accidentally goes back, the processing state and progress are lost.

This feature will introduce a processing queue and job history system to improve reliability and user experience.

## Problem It Solves
- Users lose track of image processing if they navigate away or refresh the page
- No visibility of ongoing or previously processed images
- No way to retry failed processing jobs
- Poor UX for long-running AI tasks

## Proposed Solution
- Add a **Queue tab/section** in the UI
- Store each image processing request as a job with status:
  - Pending
  - Processing
  - Completed
  - Failed
- Show real-time status updates for active jobs
- Persist job history using local storage or backend (depending on architecture)
- Allow users to:
  - View ongoing jobs
  - Reopen completed results
  - Retry failed jobs

## Additional Context
This improvement will make the AI workflow more reliable and user-friendly, especially for longer processing tasks. It will also help users avoid losing work due to accidental navigation or page refresh.

I would like to work on this feature under GSSoC 2026 if it is approved. 🚀

title: [BUG] Remove Duplicate FormData Declaration in Image Processing Controller
body:
## Describe the bug

The image processing controller contains a duplicate declaration of the `form` variable using `const`.

```js
const form = new FormData();
const form = new FormData();
```

Since `const` variables cannot be redeclared within the same scope, the application throws a syntax error during startup or compilation, preventing the backend from running correctly.

## To Reproduce

1. Clone the repository.
2. Install dependencies.
3. Start the backend server.
4. Observe the compilation/startup process.
5. The application fails with a duplicate variable declaration error originating from `backend/src/controllers/image.controller.js`.

## Expected behavior

The backend should start successfully without syntax errors, and the image processing endpoint should initialize normally.

## Screenshots

Example:

```bash
SyntaxError: Identifier 'form' has already been declared
```

- OS: Windows/macOS/Linux
- Browser: N/A
- Version: N/A

`backend/src/controllers/image.controller.js`

Bumps [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios) from 1.16.1 to 1.17.0.
Release notes

Sourced from axios's releases.

v1.17.0 — June 1, 2026

This release adds Node HTTP zstd decompression, hardens config and release workflows, and fixes authentication, header, proxy, and type-handling regressions.

🔒 Security Fixes

  • Config Hardening: Guarded socketPath, params, and paramsSerializer reads with own-property checks to prevent inherited prototype values from affecting request behavior, including SSRF-sensitive paths. (#10901, #10922)
  • Release Publishing: Switched the publish workflow to npm staged publishing for safer, auditable package releases with provenance. (#10926)

🚀 New Features

  • HTTP Compression: Added Node HTTP adapter support for zstd response decompression, with transitional.advertiseZstdAcceptEncoding controlling whether zstd is advertised in Accept-Encoding. (#6792, #10920)

🐛 Bug Fixes

  • Authentication Handling: Restored Basic auth on same-origin Node redirects while continuing to strip credentials cross-origin, and aligned the fetch adapter with HTTP adapter behavior for URL-embedded Basic auth. (#10929, #10896)
  • Proxy TLS: Preserved user httpsAgent TLS options when tunneling HTTPS requests through HTTP CONNECT proxies. (#10957)
  • React Native FormData: Cleared default Content-Type for React Native FormData so multipart boundaries can be generated correctly. (#10898)
  • Headers: Silently skipped empty or whitespace-only header names instead of throwing, matching parsed-header behavior and avoiding React Native response crashes. (#10875)
  • Request Data Merging: Preserved enumerable symbol keys when cloning plain request data through axios merge logic. (#10812)
  • Bundler Compatibility: Converted resolveConfig from an arrow default export to a named function export to avoid webpack and Babel transform interop failures. (#10891)
  • Types: Corrected AxiosHeaders.toJSON() return types and updated CommonJS isCancel typings to narrow to CanceledError<T>. (#10956, #10952)
  • Build Tooling: Avoided emitting a null Authorization header from the GitHub build helper when GITHUB_TOKEN is unset. (#10931)

🔧 Maintenance & Chores

  • HTTP/2 Internals: Extracted Http2Sessions into its own helper module and added direct unit coverage for session pooling, timeout, and cleanup behavior. (#10861)
  • Package Publishing: Reduced published package size by switching to a files allowlist and dropping unneeded unminified bundle source maps. (#10939)
  • CI and Release Automation: Added bundle-size reporting, moved reports to the job summary, fixed bundle-size comparison coverage, added Node 26 to the matrix, pinned npm for staged publishing, and prepared the 1.17.0 release. (#10907, #10911, #10916, #10927, #10935, #10983)
  • Developer Workflow: Added a dev container and iterated on OpenSpec workflow files before removing them from the release branch. (#10925, #10914, #10958)

Bumps [fuse.js](https://github.com/krisk/Fuse) from 7.3.0 to 7.4.2.
Release notes

Sourced from fuse.js's releases.

v7.4.2

Bug Fixes

  • types: ship CommonJS type declarations (.d.cts) so consumers on moduleResolution: node16/nodenext no longer hit TS1479 ("masquerading as ESM") when importing the package from a CommonJS project. The lib entries now resolve to a runtime-accurate export = declaration and the worker to its named declaration, via the require exports condition. (#780)

Full Changelog: https://github.com/krisk/Fuse/compare/v7.4.1...v7.4.2

v7.4.1

Bug Fixes

  • types: ship TypeScript declarations for fuse.js/worker (572ad1e), closes #828
  • types: add TypeScript declarations for fuse.js/worker-script (6ef6c33), closes #828

Both worker subpaths now resolve types under node16/nodenext and bundler module resolution.

v7.4.0

First stable on the 7.4 line. Aggregates everything from the 7.4.0-beta.1 → 7.4.0-beta.8 cycle. See CHANGELOG.md for the per-beta breakdown.

Features

  • FuseWorker — parallel search via Web Workers (9ba192c). New FuseWorker class shards the collection across workers, runs searches in parallel, and preserves the same result ordering as Fuse. See the Web Workers guide.
  • token-search: tokenMatch: 'all' | 'any' option for AND/OR semantics (#827, 9f979d0). Default 'any' preserves existing behavior; 'all' requires every query word to match somewhere in a record (term-centric AND), useful for filter-style queries.
  • token-search: customizable tokenizer with unicode-aware default (#821, 8e55cae). New tokenize?: RegExp | (text: string) => string[] option; the default regex now segments CJK, Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic, etc. out of the box.

Bug Fixes

  • matches: report array-path keys as dotted strings (acd54e8). Previously match.key leaked a raw string[] for keys declared as path arrays (e.g. keys: [['author', 'firstName']]), contradicting the documented FuseResultMatch.key: string type. Now emits the canonical dotted string ('author.firstName'). Behavior change: consumers relying on the array shape need to update.
  • bitap: restrict highlight indices to matched window (#792, 622f105). Closes #505, #611, #691, #793.
  • index: correct doc-index alignment for Fuse<string> with blank docs (0b8e3ca).
  • core: invalidate searcher cache on collection mutation (fcf4228).
  • token-search: renumber inverted index after doc removal (ea9356d).
  • workers: preserve global refIndex across shards (e4217f9), match Fuse ordering and reject non-cloneable options (d571390), reject useTokenSearch in FuseWorker (8c6183d).
  • match: explicitly reject useTokenSearch in Fuse.match (3959d91).
  • correct fieldCount accounting and add reverse lookup in inverted index (54e702c).
  • guard against empty-string crash in fieldNorm (e550ab1); skip consecutive spaces in fieldNorm word counting (5929af6).
  • types: resolve typecheck errors and align KeyStore types (dbc115d).
  • docs: reflect data edits in playground demo (#825, 6eff909).

Performance

  • reuse bit arrays in Bitap search instead of allocating per error level (ec9b446)
  • replace forEach with for loops in search hot paths (1945f49)
  • fast-path Math.pow when exponent is 1 in computeScore (c82de57)
  • pre-allocate records array and use for loops in FuseIndex.create (5800036)
  • replace regex with loop for word counting in fieldNorm (5517a9b)
  • use filter instead of reverse-splice in removeAll / remove (c74823b, 8372b0a)
  • token-search: drop unused postings from inverted index (5ea216f)

Internal

body:
Bumps [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios) from 1.16.1 to 1.17.0.
Release notes

Sourced from axios's releases.

v1.17.0 — June 1, 2026

This release adds Node HTTP zstd decompression, hardens config and release workflows, and fixes authentication, header, proxy, and type-handling regressions.

🔒 Security Fixes

  • Config Hardening: Guarded socketPath, params, and paramsSerializer reads with own-property checks to prevent inherited prototype values from affecting request behavior, including SSRF-sensitive paths. (#10901, #10922)
  • Release Publishing: Switched the publish workflow to npm staged publishing for safer, auditable package releases with provenance. (#10926)

🚀 New Features

  • HTTP Compression: Added Node HTTP adapter support for zstd response decompression, with transitional.advertiseZstdAcceptEncoding controlling whether zstd is advertised in Accept-Encoding. (#6792, #10920)

🐛 Bug Fixes

  • Authentication Handling: Restored Basic auth on same-origin Node redirects while continuing to strip credentials cross-origin, and aligned the fetch adapter with HTTP adapter behavior for URL-embedded Basic auth. (#10929, #10896)
  • Proxy TLS: Preserved user httpsAgent TLS options when tunneling HTTPS requests through HTTP CONNECT proxies. (#10957)
  • React Native FormData: Cleared default Content-Type for React Native FormData so multipart boundaries can be generated correctly. (#10898)
  • Headers: Silently skipped empty or whitespace-only header names instead of throwing, matching parsed-header behavior and avoiding React Native response crashes. (#10875)
  • Request Data Merging: Preserved enumerable symbol keys when cloning plain request data through axios merge logic. (#10812)
  • Bundler Compatibility: Converted resolveConfig from an arrow default export to a named function export to avoid webpack and Babel transform interop failures. (#10891)
  • Types: Corrected AxiosHeaders.toJSON() return types and updated CommonJS isCancel typings to narrow to CanceledError<T>. (#10956, #10952)
  • Build Tooling: Avoided emitting a null Authorization header from the GitHub build helper when GITHUB_TOKEN is unset. (#10931)

🔧 Maintenance & Chores

  • HTTP/2 Internals: Extracted Http2Sessions into its own helper module and added direct unit coverage for session pooling, timeout, and cleanup behavior. (#10861)
  • Package Publishing: Reduced published package size by switching to a files allowlist and dropping unneeded unminified bundle source maps. (#10939)
  • CI and Release Automation: Added bundle-size reporting, moved reports to the job summary, fixed bundle-size comparison coverage, added Node 26 to the matrix, pinned npm for staged publishing, and prepared the 1.17.0 release. (#10907, #10911, #10916, #10927, #10935, #10983)
  • Developer Workflow: Added a dev container and iterated on OpenSpec workflow files before removing them from the release branch. (#10925, #10914, #10958)
  • Documentation and Policy: Updated disclosure, contributor, collaboration, threat-model, advanced docs, README badges, release notes, moderator configuration, and project metadata. (#10890, #10889, #10921, #10945, #10905, #10933, #10915, #10887, #10955)
  • Dependencies: Bumped Babel tooling, Commitlint, ESLint, Rollup, Globals, Vitest, Playwright, fs-extra, qs, docs dependencies, and GitHub Actions dependencies including actions/dependency-review-action and zizmorcore/zizmor-action. (#10871, #10879, #10918, #10919, #10934, #10947, #10954, #10960)

🌟 New Contributors

We are thrilled to welcome our new contributors. Thank you for helping improve axios:

  • @​BasixKOR (#6792)
  • @​carladams1299-lab (#10861)
  • @​LaplaceYoung (#10812)
  • @​JamieMagee (#10939)
  • @​RonGamzu (#10905)
  • @​sapirbaruch (#10891)
  • @​nezukoagent (#10901)
  • @​devareddy05 (#10929)
  • @​Mohammad-Faiz-Cloud-Engineer (#10922)
  • @​azandabot (#10931)
  • @​niksy (#10896)
